response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "I've always been a bit skeptical about everything that pops up online, especially since I started paying attention to social media for things like job opportunities or community news. As someone who works as a cleaner in Washington DC, I don't spend too much time scrolling through feeds—maybe an hour here and there when my daughter isn't around watching her own shows—but whenever something strange catches my eye, I'm quick to question it because of that creative side of me.\n\nThere was this one time I saw an ad claiming you could clean your house in under five minutes with some miracle product; sounded too good to be true and probably fake. Instead of just believing or sharing it like most people do when they see something sensational, I'd pause and think about how that makes no sense—it takes effort to clean properly.\n\nFor dealing with fake content specifically? Well beyond ads, there's always misinformation spreading fast on these platforms because everyone thinks they're authorities online. I'm not an expert by any means since I only have my work experience backing me up—but still—my approach relies more on common sense than formal knowledge.\n\nWhen something feels off or exaggerated compared to what I know from real life (like if it's overly technical without context), I keep looking until I find evidence that contradicts it. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
I've always been a bit skeptical about everything that pops up online, especially since I started paying attention to social media for things like job opportunities or community news. As someone who works as a cleaner in Washington DC, I don't spend too much time scrolling through feeds—maybe an hour here and there when my daughter isn't around watching her own shows—but whenever something strange catches my eye, I'm quick to question it because of that creative side of me.

There was this one time I saw an ad claiming you could clean your house in under five minutes with some miracle product; sounded too good to be true and probably fake. Instead of just believing or sharing it like most people do when they see something sensational, I'd pause and think about how that makes no sense—it takes effort to clean properly.

For dealing with fake content specifically? Well beyond ads, there's always misinformation spreading fast on these platforms because everyone thinks they're authorities online. I'm not an expert by any means since I only have my work experience backing me up—but still—my approach relies more on common sense than formal knowledge.

When something feels off or exaggerated compared to what I know from real life (like if it's overly technical without context), I keep looking until I find evidence that contradicts it. If it doesn't hold water, then it’s probably fake and shouldn’t be believed or passed along.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
1. Start by questioning the validity of claims through critical thinking.
2. Look for supporting evidence before accepting anything as true.
3. Utilize common sense to evaluate how realistic something is based on real-world experience.
4. Avoid spreading content without verification due to creative skepticism.
response_parsed.tone: Skeptical yet practical, relying on everyday life experiences rather than formal education or expertise.
